reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016): documented demand for reducing administrative/bureaucratic burden imposed on teachers and schools, and for genuine professional consultation before policy changes affecting classroom and administrative practice, instead of top-down ministry design.
reactions.interest: Practicing teachers and school-level staff who will have to administer the new assessment and absorb the monitoring burden, represented by the CKP/Tanítanék movement.
reactions.public_good_frame: An admissions reform can only deliver its equity goal if the schools and teachers who run it are resourced and consulted, not merely handed new mandates — an overloaded, unconsulted teaching workforce undermines the policy's own aims.
reactions.argument: The scenario's own cost categories add a 'recurring compliance-audit and monitoring mechanism' explicitly flagged as omitted from the original cost list, and a multi-year psychometric assessment redesign — both of which fall on schools and teachers to implement — while every implementation step names only Ministry, Oktatási Hivatal, Klebelsberg Centre and the Legislator, with no described role for teacher professional bodies in designing the assessment or the quota mechanics they will administer. That matches the top-down, consultation-free pattern the movement organized against.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if assessment design and quota implementation included formal teacher/school professional consultation, and if the compliance-audit burden were resourced with dedicated non-teaching staff rather than added to existing teacher workload.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016) / Tanítanék javaslatcsomag (2026): documented demand that any new pedagogical mandate be preceded or accompanied by resolution of the chronic teacher shortage, adequate pay, and a real career model — not layered onto an unresolved staffing crisis.
reactions.interest: Teachers who would be expected to staff the 'advanced-programme funding in general schools' the scenario relies on to retain ambitious families in the common track.
reactions.public_good_frame: Better peer composition in general schools is only real if general-school teachers are actually able and supported enough to deliver it — funding a programme line does not by itself solve a supply problem.
reactions.argument: The scenario's own mechanism section calls general schools' ability to 'absorb and challenge high-achieving pupils' contested, and its uncertainties section names 'current teacher shortages' as the central open question determining feasibility. A funding formula for advanced tracks that does not also address pay, career progression and administrative load risks becoming exactly the kind of unfunded mandate on stretched teachers that the movement has mobilized against.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if advanced-programme funding is paired with concrete, binding teacher-supply measures (pay, career model, reduced administrative load) sufficient to actually staff the promised programmes, and if teacher representative bodies have input into how the programmes are designed and rolled out.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016): documented demand for professional autonomy over pedagogical methods, and for teacher working conditions/support (class size, special-education support, career model) to be treated as a first-order commitment rather than an afterthought to structural reorganization; also documented opposition to centrally imposed change without teacher buy-in (the movement's core complaint against KLIK-era centralization).
reactions.interest: Teachers who would be required to deliver 'differentiated instruction' in comprehensive classrooms and absorb nationwide retraining, represented by the CKP/Tanítanék movement.
reactions.public_good_frame: A comprehensive-school reform only compresses the SES gradient if teachers are genuinely equipped and supported to teach mixed-ability classrooms — without that, removing the selection point just relocates the problem into under-resourced classrooms.
reactions.argument: The scenario's own evidence status concedes that Finland's equity gains were 'bundled with teacher-training and special-education reform, not tracking removal alone,' yet its cost section admits 'no numeric range or per-cohort/per-district unit cost currently available' for that retraining, and the standardized special-education support this depends on is flagged as 'omitted from the original cost list.' A structural conversion planned and legislated by Ministry and Legislator, with retraining designed 'for' rather than 'with' teachers, repeats the top-down pattern the movement organized against — and risks leaving teachers to carry an unfunded pedagogical burden if the bundled supports it depends on are not actually delivered.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if teacher retraining and support-staff commitments are codesigned with teacher representative bodies, bindingly funded (not merely costed as 'high, no estimate'), and if the underlying pay/shortage crisis is addressed running ahead of or alongside rollout rather than after it.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016): documented demands include (a) higher teacher salaries and a transparent career/recruitment model, especially in hard-to-staff schools, and (b) restoration of school- and teacher-level autonomy over pedagogical methods and curriculum/textbook choice, against centralized (KLIK-era) prescriptive control.
reactions.interest: Teachers in disadvantaged general schools who are the direct targets of the premia/recruitment package, and teachers generally with respect to their autonomy over curriculum and methods.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation for disadvantaged schools only works if it actually solves the staffing problem rather than just naming an incentive, and instructional quality depends on teacher professional judgment being trusted, not on uniform prescription from the centre.
reactions.argument: The scenario's teacher-premia and recruitment-incentive elements for disadvantaged schools align with the movement's long-standing pay/career-model demand, but the scenario's own political-risk section admits 'money alone cannot manufacture qualified teachers in a shortage market' and that compensation budgets are 'the first cut in fiscal consolidation' — precisely the durability problem the movement has flagged about promises that aren't legally anchored. Separately, the 'rigorous, standardized national curriculum expectations' component pulls toward more centralized prescription of instructional content, which is in tension with the documented demand for restored teacher autonomy over methods and materials rather than a return to top-down curriculum control.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support the pay/recruitment and disadvantaged-school-funding elements if legally protected from first-round budget cuts and delivered at the scale promised; support for the curriculum-standardization element is conditional on it setting outcome expectations rather than prescribing methods or textbooks, preserving genuine school- and teacher-level pedagogical autonomy.
